article_title: There's a free way to use Claude Code — and it's surprisingly simple
core_thesis: Claude Code, Anthropic's agentic coding tool, is free and open-source; the cost comes from API calls to frontier models. By pointing Claude Code to a local model via Ollama, users can run it entirely for free on their own hardware, though performance depends on local compute and model quality.
detailed_summary: The article explains that Claude Code is not a standalone AI but a harness that orchestrates file editing, terminal commands, and context management, delegating reasoning to a language model. The harness itself is free and open-source; the expense is the API call to models like Sonnet or Opus. To bypass this, the author describes using Ollama to run local open-weight models (e.g., Gemma 4, Qwen3.6) and configuring Claude Code to use them via the --model flag and ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL environment variable. The setup is straightforward: install Ollama, pull a model, and launch Claude Code with 'ollama launch claude'. The article acknowledges that local models are less capable than frontier models but notes the gap is shrinking for everyday tasks. Hardware requirements are significant: Apple Silicon Macs with 32GB+ unified memory or high-VRAM GPUs are recommended; 16GB machines can run smaller quantized models like Gemma 4 E4B (5GB in 4-bit). The author personally runs a separate inference server with Ollama and uses it as a fallback to save tokens. Reader comments highlight that Claude Code's source is not officially open-source (though leaked), and alternatives like Continue.dev or OpenCode exist. Some users report issues with tool calls using Gemma 4, while Qwen3.5 works well. The article concludes that this free setup is worth trying for those on the fence about a Pro subscription.

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.role: Anthropic's agentic coding tool that runs in the terminal, managing file edits and commands.

entities.name: Ollama
entities.role: Tool for running open-weight LLMs locally, providing a local API endpoint.

entities.name: Gemma 4
entities.role: Google DeepMind's open-weight model family, with a 26B MoE variant suitable for local use.

entities.name: Qwen3.6
entities.role: Alibaba's open-weight model optimized for agentic coding, available in 27B and 35B variants.

entities.name: Anthropic
entities.role: Creator of Claude Code and Claude models.

entities.name: Raghav Sethi
entities.role: Author of the article, tech writer at MakeUseOf.

key_data_points: Claude Code is free and open-source; the cost is from API calls to frontier models like Sonnet/Opus (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: you can run it locally without incurring API costs, useful for sensitive or offline environments).

By setting the ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL environment variable to a local Ollama endpoint, Claude Code can use any local model instead of Anthropic's API (🟢 relevant to your DevOps/Homelab: this is a simple configuration trick to integrate local LLMs into your toolchain).

Recommended local models: Qwen3.6 (27B/35B, ~17-24GB RAM) and Gemma 4 (26B MoE, 4B active parameters, or 31B dense variant) (🟢 relevant to your programming: these models are specifically built for coding tasks and can be run on consumer hardware).

Hardware requirements: Apple Silicon Mac with 32GB+ unified memory or a GPU with high VRAM; 16GB machines can run smaller quantized models like Gemma 4 E4B (~5GB in 4-bit) (🟢 relevant to your Homelab: plan your hardware accordingly; a dedicated inference server is a viable homelab project).

Reader comments note that Claude Code's source is not officially open-source (only leaked), and alternatives like Continue.dev (VS Code extension) and OpenCode exist (🟢 relevant to your programming: consider these alternatives if you prefer a different IDE integration).

Some users report that Gemma 4 fails on tool calls with Claude Code, while Qwen3.5 works well; context window size must be set to at least 64K in Ollama for decent prompts (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: test model compatibility before relying on a setup).
methodology_steps: Install Ollama from ollama.com and ensure it's running as a local server.
Pull a compatible coding model: run 'ollama pull gemma4' or 'ollama pull qwen3.6' (choose based on your hardware).
Set the environment variable ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to your Ollama endpoint (default: http://localhost:11434).
Launch Claude Code with the --model flag pointing to your local model: 'ollama launch claude' (or use the Claude Code CLI directly with the model flag).
Optionally, configure a larger context window in Ollama (e.g., 64K tokens) by setting OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH or modifying the model's parameters.
Test the setup with a simple coding task to verify tool calls (file editing, command execution) work correctly.
conclusions: Claude Code can be used for free by pairing it with local models via Ollama, eliminating API costs. This makes agentic coding accessible without a subscription, though performance depends on local hardware and model quality.

Local models like Qwen3.6 and Gemma 4 are closing the gap with frontier models for everyday coding tasks, but they still fall short for complex reasoning. The trade-off is acceptable for quick tasks or when saving tokens.

The setup is straightforward and leverages existing open-source tools, making it a practical addition to a developer's toolkit. It also enables offline or private coding assistance, which is valuable for security-conscious workflows.
personal_relevance_summary: This article directly addresses your interest in free, self-hosted AI coding tools. As a programmer, you can set up Claude Code with local models to get agentic assistance without recurring costs—ideal for homelab environments or offline work. For Red Teaming, running models locally ensures sensitive code never leaves your machine. The hardware requirements also give you a concrete homelab project: building a dedicated inference server. The reader comments highlight potential pitfalls (model compatibility, context window size) that you should test before relying on the setup.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a dedicated inference server (e.g., a used workstation with a high-VRAM GPU) running Ollama and expose it to your LAN. This gives you a persistent, always-on coding assistant that you can access from any device. Also, experiment with different models (Qwen3.6 for coding, Gemma 4 for general tasks) to find the best fit for your workflow. If you encounter tool call failures, try adjusting the model's prompt format or using a different model version.
